annotations.label: Arithmetical hierarchy

anchor.snippet: classifies certain sets based on the complexity of formulas that define them

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of the arithmetical hierarchy Σⁿ/Πⁿ/Δⁿ for sets of naturals.

annotations.label: Arithmetical set

anchor.snippet: Any set that receives a classification is called arithmetical

annotations.note: No notion of `Arithmetical` set is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounded-quantifier formula classification

anchor.snippet: is logically equivalent to a formula having no unbounded quantifiers

annotations.note: Mathlib has `IsQF` (quantifier-free) and `IsAtomic` for first-order formulas but no Δ₀/bounded-quantifier class specific to arithmetic.

annotations.label: Inductive classifications Σn and Πn for formulas

anchor.snippet: are defined inductively for every natural number n using the following rules

annotations.note: No inductive Σₙ/Πₙ classification of formulas exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Σn/Πn quantifier alternation form

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to a formula that begins with some existential quantifiers and alternates

annotations.note: Mathlib has prenex normal form (`toPrenex`) but no theorem characterising the Σₙ/Πₙ alternation pattern.

annotations.label: Every formula receives a classification

anchor.snippet: Because every first-order formula has a prenex normal form

annotations.note: Mathlib proves every first-order formula has a prenex normal form via `toPrenex_isPrenex`, but does not turn this into a Σₙ/Πₙ classification.

annotations.label: Set defined by a formula

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers
anchor.snippet: is defined by a formula φ in the language of Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Set.Definable` for arbitrary first-order languages/structures but not specialised to ℕ in the language of Peano arithmetic.

annotations.label: Definable in first-order arithmetic

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers
anchor.snippet: A set is definable in first-order arithmetic if it is defined by some formula in the language of Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: No predicate for `definable in the language of Peano arithmetic` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classifications Σn, Πn, Δn of sets

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers
anchor.snippet: is assigned classifications of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Σₙ/Πₙ/Δₙ classification of sets of natural numbers.

annotations.label: Odd natural numbers

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers
anchor.snippet: the set of odd natural numbers

annotations.note: The odd numbers exist in Mathlib (`Odd`), but no result places them at level Σ₀ of an arithmetical hierarchy.

annotations.label: Arithmetical hierarchy on k-tuples

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers
anchor.snippet: A parallel definition is used to define the arithmetical hierarchy on finite Cartesian powers of the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: No arithmetical hierarchy on ℕᵏ in Mathlib since the unary version is not formalised.

annotations.label: Meaning of subscript n

anchor.snippet: indicates the number of alternations of blocks of universal and existential first-order quantifiers

annotations.note: No Σₙ/Πₙ subscript notation is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Meaning of superscript (type)

anchor.snippet: indicates the type of the objects being quantified over

annotations.note: No Σⁱₙ/Πⁱₙ superscript notation for object-type is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Σ1 sets are recursively enumerable

anchor.snippet: These are exactly the recursively enumerable sets

annotations.note: Mathlib defines recursively enumerable predicates via `REPred` but has no Σ₁ class to identify with.

annotations.label: Indices of total Turing machines are Π2

anchor.snippet: The set of natural numbers that are indices for Turing machines that compute total functions

annotations.note: No statement about indices of total recursive functions being Π₂ exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effectively open and effectively closed sets

anchor.snippet: subset of Baire space or Cantor space is an open set in the usual topology

annotations.note: No notion of effectively open/closed subset of Cantor or Baire space is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arithmetical sets are Borel

anchor.snippet: Every arithmetical subset of Cantor space or Baire space is a Borel set

annotations.note: Mathlib has Borel sets but no arithmetical hierarchy and so no relation between the two.

annotations.label: Bounded-quantifier formulas decidable in E

anchor.snippet: formulas can be checked by going over all cases one by one, which is possible because all their quantifiers are bounded

annotations.note: No decidability-in-E result for bounded-quantifier formulas is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Alternative definition with primitive recursive functions

anchor.snippet: formulas under an alternative definition, that allows the use of primitive recursive functions with bounded quantifiers

annotations.note: No alternative arithmetical-hierarchy definition using primitive recursive function symbols is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relativized arithmetical hierarchy

anchor.snippet: we can extend this notion to the whole arithmetic hierarchy

annotations.note: No relativised arithmetical hierarchy is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Numbers divisible by an element of Y

anchor.snippet: Let X be the set of numbers divisible by an element of Y

annotations.note: No example placing `{n | ∃ y ∈ Y, y ∣ n}` in a relativised hierarchy exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arithmetical reducibility

anchor.snippet: Arithmetical reducibility is an intermediate notion between Turing reducibility and hyperarithmetic reducibility

annotations.note: Arithmetical reducibility is not defined in Mathlib (Mathlib only has Turing reducibility via `TuringReducible`).

annotations.label: Arithmetical set / arithmetical in Y

anchor.snippet: A set is arithmetical (also arithmetic and arithmetically definable ) if it is defined by some formula in the language of Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of `arithmetical set` or `arithmetical in Y`.

annotations.label: Arithmetic degrees

anchor.snippet: The equivalence classes of this relation are called the arithmetic degrees

annotations.note: Arithmetic degrees are not defined in Mathlib (only `TuringDegree` exists).

annotations.label: Cantor space and Baire space

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: is the set of all infinite sequences of 0s and 1s

annotations.note: Mathlib has Cantor space as ℕ → Bool / ℕ → Fin 2 via the standard product topology, but no first-class `BaireSpace = ℕ → ℕ` definition tied to descriptive set theory.

annotations.label: Classification of subsets of Cantor space

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: A subset of Cantor space is assigned the classification

annotations.note: No Σⁿ/Πⁿ classification of subsets of Cantor space exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-empty sets of natural numbers as Σ1 subset of Cantor space

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: the set of all infinite binary strings that aren't all 0

annotations.note: No formalisation of this Σ₁⁰ example exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two hierarchies on Cantor space differ

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: these are not the same hierarchy

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalise either hierarchy on Cantor space, so no comparison theorem exists.

annotations.label: Arithmetical hierarchy on Baire space via Cantor correspondence

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: A subset of Baire space has a corresponding subset of Cantor space under the map that takes each function

annotations.note: No transfer of an arithmetical hierarchy from Cantor space to Baire space is formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Functional second-order definition on Baire space

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: An equivalent definition of the arithmetical hierarchy on Baire space

annotations.note: No second-order definition of the arithmetical hierarchy on Baire space exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hierarchy on Cartesian powers and effective Polish spaces

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: A parallel definition is used to define the arithmetical hierarchy on finite Cartesian powers of Baire space or Cantor space

annotations.note: No arithmetical hierarchy on products of Cantor/Baire space or on effective Polish spaces in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Boldface arithmetic hierarchy = Borel hierarchy

anchor.section: The arithmetical hierarchy of subsets of Cantor and Baire space
anchor.snippet: we can also define the arithmetic hierarchy of subsets of the Cantor and Baire spaces relative to some set of natural numbers

annotations.note: Neither a boldface arithmetical hierarchy nor a Borel hierarchy is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hierarchy with primitive recursive function symbols

anchor.snippet: It is possible to define the arithmetical hierarchy of formulas using a language extended with a function symbol for each primitive recursive function

annotations.note: This variant arithmetical hierarchy is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semantic hierarchy on finitary relations

anchor.snippet: A more semantic variation of the hierarchy can be defined on all finitary relations on the natural numbers

annotations.note: No semantic variant of the arithmetical hierarchy on finitary relations is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Closure under finite unions and intersections

anchor.snippet: are closed under finite unions and finite intersections of their respective elements

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Σₙ/Πₙ classes to prove closure properties about.

annotations.label: Complement and Δn characterization

anchor.snippet: if and only if its complement is

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Σₙ/Πₙ/Δₙ to formulate this characterisation.

annotations.label: Strict inclusions; hierarchy does not collapse

anchor.snippet: Thus the hierarchy does not collapse

annotations.note: No non-collapse theorem for the arithmetical hierarchy is formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inclusions Σn, Πn ⊆ Δn+1

anchor.snippet: The inclusions

annotations.note: These hierarchy-inclusion lemmas are not in Mathlib since the hierarchy isn't defined.

annotations.label: Halts-on-n-not-on-m pair set

anchor.snippet: the set of pairs ( n , m ) such that T halts on n but not on m

annotations.note: The Σ₁ ∩ Π₁ \ (Σ₀ ∪ Π₀) example pair set is not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Computable sets are recursively enumerable with r.e. complement

anchor.snippet: then both S and its complement are recursively enumerable

annotations.note: `computable_iff_re_compl_re` (and its classical companion `computable_iff_re_compl_re'`) says a predicate is computable iff both it and its complement are r.e.

annotations.label: Computable sets are Δ1

anchor.snippet: By Post's theorem , both S and its complement are in

annotations.note: Mathlib has `ComputablePred.to_re` (computable ⇒ r.e.) and its application to the complement, but no Δ₁ class to land in.

annotations.label: Δ1 sets are computable

anchor.snippet: Similarly, for every set S in

annotations.note: Mathlib proves r.e. ∧ co-r.e. ⇒ computable, which is the content of Δ₁ ⇒ computable, but without the Δ₁ label.

annotations.label: Turing computable = Δ1; r.e. = Σ1

anchor.snippet: The Turing computable sets of natural numbers are exactly the sets at level

annotations.note: Mathlib has `ComputablePred` and `REPred` and their relationship, but no Δ₁/Σ₁ labels to state the equivalence formally.

annotations.label: Halting problem for oracle

anchor.snippet: No oracle machine is capable of solving its own halting problem

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the unrelativised halting problem (`halting_problem`) but not the relativised version for arbitrary oracles.

annotations.label: Post's theorem

anchor.snippet: Post's theorem establishes a close connection between the arithmetical hierarchy of sets of natural numbers and the Turing degrees

annotations.note: Mathlib formalises the n = 0 case of Post's theorem (computable ⇔ r.e. ∧ co-r.e.) but not the full hierarchy-versus-Turing-jumps statement.

annotations.label: Polynomial hierarchy as resource-bounded analog

anchor.snippet: The polynomial hierarchy is a "feasible resource-bounded" version of the arithmetical hierarchy

annotations.note: The polynomial hierarchy is not defined in Mathlib.
